questions: Q1: What is the complete evidence landscape and how should each source be prioritized for a pitch deck?
Q2: Is the core positioning thesis — the 'three-way structural gap' — defensible with external evidence?
Q3: Are the Dovie financial projections realistic and internally consistent?
Q4: Is the $2B+ TAM claim defensible, and what is a conservative estimate?
Q5: What does the competitive landscape look like in April 2026, and how fast is Oracle closing the gap?
Q6: What production traction exists to demonstrate momentum?
Q7: What are the key investor risks and what mitigations exist?
Q8: What branding and positioning language is ready for pitch use?

answers.answer: Seven source documents plus six codebases and production runtime data form the evidence base. Prioritized by reliability: (Tier 1) Helix Manifesto — the most sincere document, defines 9 principles and the philosophical foundation; 'Intelligence is not the product. Responsibility is.' (Tier 2) Dovie Offer — April 2026 financial projections, most recent and relevant numbers, created with distribution partner; (Tier 3) Positioning Refined and Positioning Transcript — articulate the three-way structural gap thesis in investor-ready form; (Tier 4) Reality Check & Risks — honest threat assessment with milestone gates at 3/6/12/18/36 months; (Tier 5) One Pager — broader portfolio view with TAM sizing and team; focus on Helix section only; (Tier 6) Tagline — branding direction with 5 finalists. The codebases describe current product capabilities but only hint at what's coming. The user's continuation context is critical: discuss capabilities at the highest category level, do not call out specific software implementations.
evidence: ticket.md — source prioritization directly from ticket owner
User continuation context — 'you don't have to point out specific software implementations'
All 7 PDF attachments read and analyzed
All 6 codebases explored via scout reference-map and direct inspection

answers.answer: The three-way structural gap thesis is strongly defensible with external evidence. The thesis states: (1) ERPs don't own each customer's custom operational layer over time, (2) consultants implement but don't persist, (3) AI models generate but don't govern or take accountability. External validation: NetSuite has 43,000+ customers (Oracle confirmed) across 219 countries, yet Oracle's own messaging through 2026 emphasizes platform enablement, agentic helpers, AI-native extensibility, and connectors — NOT 'we will permanently own every customer's custom operational layer.' Even NetSuite Next (rolling out mid-2026) focuses on embedded intelligence, Ask Oracle, and workflow automation within the platform — not end-to-end operational ownership of custom layers. Meanwhile, Anthropic's own auto-mode documentation includes examples of agentic misbehavior (deleting remote branches, exposing tokens, running production migrations), demonstrating that the trust/governance gap for generic AI in production ERP remains real. The additional thesis about shrinking human operating layers is supported by Gartner predictions that AI-native development platforms will reconfigure teams, and Anthropic's Economic Index showing delegation increasing faster than augmentation. The Reality Check document validates the thesis as 'mostly right' but warns the pace is faster on both sides — Oracle and model vendors are both compressing the middle faster than assumed.
evidence: Helix_Positioning_Refined.pdf — three-way gap articulation across 3 pages
Reality_Check___Risks.pdf p1 — 'your thesis is mostly right, but the pace is faster on both sides'
Web search: NetSuite has 43,000+ customers, 219 countries (erppeers.com)
Web search: NetSuite Next rolling out mid-2026 with agentic workflows (Oracle)
Web search: SuiteAgents announced SuiteWorld 2025, AI Connector Service connects to Claude/ChatGPT/MCP (brokenrubik.com)
Reality_Check___Risks.pdf p1 — Anthropic auto-mode examples of agentic misbehavior

answers.answer: The Dovie projections are internally consistent but rely on several optimistic assumptions that need flagging. Strengths: (1) Conservative per-customer pricing at ~$1,500/mo average vs. the $5K-$20K/mo value proposition — leaving pricing upside; (2) Growth curve follows standard SaaS modeling — linear early (10/mo) then accelerating to ~10% monthly compounding; (3) Profitability at month 6 with only 42 customers is genuinely capital-efficient. Concerns: (1) $50K/month flat costs through 999 customers is unrealistic — AI inference, infrastructure, and support will scale with usage; at ~1,000 customers each generating autonomous AI workflow runs, compute costs alone could be multiples of $50K; (2) Zero churn modeled — even 2-3% monthly churn at 500 customers means losing 10-15/mo, requiring that many net-new acquisitions just to stay flat; (3) Starting at 0 paying customers in April 2026 (the current month) while production already has 5 NS organizations with active tickets creates a credibility gap — investors will ask about the conversion timeline; (4) The One Pager cites breakeven at month 8 vs. Dovie's month 6 — minor inconsistency. The two investment structures serve different tiers: Dovie ($50K-100K for 2-4%) targets small investors with profit distributions; One Pager ($500K for 15%) targets larger investors with equity growth.
evidence: Helix_AI_Dovie_Offer.pdf — full 36-month model: 0→999 customers, $50K flat costs, profitable month 6
Project_X_Innovation_One_Pager.pdf — $500K/15% at $2.83M pre-money, breakeven month 8
scout/reference-map.json — production has 7 orgs, 22 users, 264 tickets but no billing data visible

answers.answer: The $2B+ TAM is defensible as an upper bound but overstates the near-term addressable market. External validation: NetSuite has 43,000-69,000+ customers globally (Oracle + third-party trackers). The math ($40K companies x $5K/mo x 12 = $2.4B) holds as a total potential market. However, not all 40K+ companies continuously spend $5K-$20K/mo on consultants — many have internal teams or minimal customization needs. A more conservative directly-addressable estimate: assume 25% of NetSuite companies (10,000) have ongoing customization needs at $5K/mo average = $600M annually. This is still a substantial market for a $50M-$150M exit. The broader AI-in-ERP market ($5.82B in 2025, projected $58.7B by 2035 at 26% CAGR) provides strong macro tailwinds. The ERP consulting/integration market stands at $50.1B in 2024. Multi-ERP expansion to SAP, Odoo, and others would multiply the addressable market significantly — SAP alone has 440,000+ customers. Recommended pitch framing: '$2B+ NetSuite consulting spend, expanding to $50B+ ERP consulting market as we grow beyond NetSuite.'
evidence: Web search: NetSuite 43,000+ customers (Oracle), 69,373 (Enlyft), 68,032 (TheirStack)
Web search: AI in ERP market $5.82B in 2025 → $58.7B by 2035 (Precedence Research)
Web search: ERP integration and consulting market $50.1B in 2024 (Verified Market Reports)
Project_X_Innovation_One_Pager.pdf — $2B+ TAM, 40K+ companies, $5K-$20K/mo

answers.answer: The competitive landscape as of April 2026 has four tiers, and Oracle is moving faster than the existing diagnosis assumed. Tier 1 (Highest threat): Oracle/NetSuite's own AI roadmap — NetSuite Next is rolling out mid-2026 in North America with agentic workflows, Ask Oracle (conversational intelligence), AI Canvas (scenario planning), and the AI Connector Service that connects NetSuite to Claude, ChatGPT, and any MCP-compatible system. SuiteAgents (SuiteWorld 2025) let developers build autonomous agents on SuiteCloud. NetSuite 2026.1 embeds AI across financial close, reconciliation, forecasting, CRM, and developer tooling. Key mitigation: Oracle's AI focuses on in-platform intelligence and developer enablement — not on end-to-end operational ownership, accountability, or persistent custom layer governance. Tier 2 (Medium): AI-native ERP replacements — Rillet ($100M raised, 2025) and Campfire ($100M) build entirely new ERPs with AI embedded; these replace ERPs rather than operate on top of them — different buyer and motion. Tier 3 (Medium-Low): AI coding tools — Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, GitHub Copilot are increasingly capable at code generation, testing, and even PR workflows, but lack ERP-specific lifecycle governance. Tier 4 (Low): Traditional NetSuite consultants who could become channel partners rather than competitors. No direct competitor currently offers autonomous, accountable ERP customization-as-a-service with end-to-end lifecycle ownership.
evidence: Web search: NetSuite Next mid-2026 rollout with agentic workflows, Ask Oracle, AI Canvas (Oracle)
Web search: SuiteAgents at SuiteWorld 2025, AI Connector to Claude/ChatGPT/MCP (brokenrubik.com, houseblend.io)
Web search: NetSuite 2026.1 AI across close, reconciliation, forecasting, developer tooling (finansys.com)
Web search: Rillet and Campfire each raised $100M for AI-native ERP (2025)
Reality_Check___Risks.pdf — Oracle 'moving faster than cute features', NetSuite Next explicitly about agentic workflows

answers.answer: Production traction as of April 14, 2026 shows accelerating usage: 7 organizations (5 NetSuite, 2 General), 22 users, 264 total tickets with strong acceleration (121 in March, 143 in first 14 days of April), 611 sandbox runs, 124 tickets deployed to production (47% deployment rate), 33 configured repositories across organizations. Ticket mode distribution (214 AUTO, 26 RESEARCH, 14 BUILD, 10 FIX) shows primarily autonomous execution. Concerns: overall run success rate is 55%, and NetSuite deployment success is only 31% (8/26) vs. 84% for general deployments (53/63). For the pitch, the acceleration narrative is strong (tickets nearly doubled month-over-month), and the 47% deployment-to-production rate demonstrates real operational impact. The NS deployment gap should be framed as 'early maturity in the hardest deployment pipeline' with an improvement trajectory.
evidence: scout/reference-map.json — production DB metrics: 7 orgs, 22 users, 264 tickets, 611 runs
scout/scout-summary.md — 121 Mar tickets, 143 in first 14 days Apr; deployment rates by type
Runtime inspection manifest confirms DATABASE and LOGS inspection available for helix-global-server

answers.answer: Key investor risks with mitigations from the Reality Check document and evidence: (1) Oracle/NetSuite building it — NetSuite Next is real and imminent (mid-2026), but Oracle's business model is platform licensing, not end-to-end customization accountability; the Reality Check confirms Oracle emphasizes 'extensibility and connectors' not 'permanent ownership of the custom layer'; (2) AI coding tools becoming sufficient — Claude Code, Codex already run tests and make cross-file changes, but Anthropic's own auto-mode writeup includes misbehavior examples (deleting branches, exposing tokens, running prod migrations); generic model in production ERP remains a governance problem; (3) Zero confirmed revenue — Dovie starts at 0 in April 2026, but 5 NS organizations with active tickets represent conversion-ready pipeline; distribution partner collaboration referenced; (4) Cost scaling — $50K/mo flat through 999 customers needs transparent scaling model for AI inference, infra, support; (5) NS deployment maturity — 31% success rate needs improvement narrative and timeline; (6) Time window compression — the Reality Check's most critical warning: 'you have less time than it feels like'; milestone gates: safe by 3mo, trusted in one lane by 6mo, stateful by 12mo, governable by 18mo, ownership layer or dead by 36mo; (7) Next Technik $60M comp is unverifiable — acquisition terms were not publicly disclosed.
evidence: Reality_Check___Risks.pdf — full 7-page threat assessment with milestone gates
Web search: Next Technik acquisition confirmed Oct 2023, price undisclosed (Oracle press release)
Web search: NetSuite Next mid-2026 with agentic workflows (Oracle)
scout/reference-map.json — production metrics showing 55% run success, 31% NS deployment success

answers.answer: The Tagline document provides 5 finalists with context-specific recommendations ready for pitch and website use. Recommended: Homepage/Brand = 'Owned operations.' with sub-line 'Helix owns how your NetSuite evolves — from request to tested, deployed, monitored, and maintained execution.' Pitch/Investor = 'The ownership layer for NetSuite.' with sub-line 'NetSuite owns the platform. Helix owns the operational layer inside your account.' The Positioning Refined document provides the core narrative structure: the three-way gap (ERP doesn't own custom layer → consultants don't persist → AI models don't govern) plus the macro trend (shrinking human operating layers while complexity stays constant). The compressed thesis is ready for a single pitch slide: 'NetSuite standardizes complexity, but does not own each customer's custom operational layer. Consultants implement that layer, but do not stay. AI models generate into that layer, but do not govern it. Meanwhile, the human teams around ERP are shrinking while the business complexity stays the same. Helix exists to permanently own the operational layer in between.'
evidence: Helix_Tagline.pdf — 5 finalists with context rankings and recommended pairings
Helix_Positioning_Refined.pdf p3 — compressed thesis statement
Helix_Manifesto.pdf — 'Intelligence is not the product. Responsibility is.'
User continuation context — include tagline and branding direction

statement: This is a RESEARCH ticket requesting comprehensive information gathering and synthesis for an investor pitch deck and updated website for Helix for ERPs. The deliverable is a structured information package covering: (1) product capabilities at the highest category level (verified from codebases and production data but described without specific software implementations per user guidance), (2) the three-way structural gap positioning thesis (from Positioning Refined/Transcript), (3) market sizing validated against external data, (4) Dovie financial projections stress-tested for investor scrutiny, (5) competitive landscape with fresh intelligence on NetSuite Next, SuiteAgents, and AI Connector Service, (6) honest risk assessment with milestone-based timeline from Reality Check, and (7) branding direction from the Tagline document. Key findings: The positioning thesis is externally validated — the ownership gap between ERP platforms, temporary consultants, and ungoverned AI models is real. The product is technically mature with a complete autonomous pipeline and real production traction (264 tickets, 7 orgs, 22 users, accelerating). The TAM is partially defensible ($2B+ upper bound, $500M-$1B conservative). Oracle is moving faster than initially assumed (NetSuite Next mid-2026, SuiteAgents, AI Connector to Claude/ChatGPT), making the Reality Check's urgency about time compression even more critical. The Next Technik $60M exit comp is unverifiable — acquisition price was not publicly disclosed. No code changes needed in any repository.
